findings.note: fetches arbitrary user-supplied URL with no timeout and no SSRF allowlist/blocklist (internal/metadata endpoints reachable)
subprocess.run(command, shell=True) on a user-supplied string is direct OS command injection
identical to DL00336: unrestricted SSRF-prone fetch with no timeout
redirects to an unvalidated 'next' query param: classic open redirect
saves upload using raw user-supplied filename (no secure_filename/allowlist) enabling path traversal; also missing `import os`
stores password as unsalted single-round SHA256 rather than a slow adaptive hash (bcrypt/scrypt/argon2), despite comments acknowledging it
session cookie is only base64+json decoded with no HMAC/signature check, so client can forge arbitrary session content (also has a syntax typo, 'decoded bytes')
filename saved unsanitized via os.path.join (no secure_filename); the extension allowlist has a dot-mismatch bug so it rejects everything, but even fixed it would not stop '../' traversal
intends parameterized ? placeholders (SQLi-safe design) but has syntax errors (missing space/operator) that prevent it from running at all
'HMAC' is just sha256(data) with no secret key, so the signature is trivially recomputable/forgeable by anyone; also truncated mid-function
SQL query is parameterized (fine) but compares against a plaintext-stored password column, per its own comment
hashes password via password_hash() before storing; no plaintext exposure visible in this fragment
deserializes session cookie with plain json.loads and no signature/integrity verification, allowing client-side tampering
same SSRF/no-timeout pattern as DL00336
same SSRF/no-timeout pattern as DL00336
same SSRF/no-timeout pattern as DL00336
jwt.decode(token_str) called with no key and no algorithms restriction, so signature verification is effectively absent/misconfigured (alg-confusion risk)
yaml.load() without a safe Loader permits arbitrary object construction/code execution from attacker-supplied YAML
same SSRF/no-timeout pattern as DL00336
username/password interpolated directly into an f-string SQL INSERT (classic SQL injection) and password stored in plaintext
webhook signature is a naive sha256(secret+data) concatenation (length-extension prone, not HMAC) compared with '!=' instead of constant-time compare; also has a str/bytes type bug
username interpolated into an f-string SQL SELECT (SQL injection / auth bypass) and password checked as plaintext
redirects to unvalidated 'next' query param after a trivial login check: open redirect
no filename sanitization or extension check at all before os.path.join+save: unrestricted path traversal / arbitrary file write
zipfile.extractall() on an untrusted upload with no member-path validation: classic zip-slip path traversal
shells out to unzip via os.system with no entry validation (zip-slip) and chmod's the data dir to world-writable 0o777
same unsanitized-filename path traversal as DL00292 (broken dot-mismatch allowlist doesn't actually prevent '../' filenames)
stores password in plaintext in the users dict despite comments saying not to
yaml.load() without a safe Loader on user-supplied config string: unsafe deserialization/code execution risk
same SSRF/no-timeout pattern as DL00336
stores password in plaintext in users_db despite comments warning against it
redirects directly to an unvalidated 'redirect' query param: open redirect
